content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 17 2017

Laurence S. Wiener
City Attorney
City of Beverly Hills
455 N. Rexford Drive, Room 230
Beverly Hills, CA 90210

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-17-018a

Dear Mr. Wiener:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vice Mayor Nancy Krasne and
Councilmember Lili Bosse regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ On February 15, 2017, you asked that we separately consider
Question 1 of Wiener Advice Letter, No. I-17-018 so that you could obtain formal advice for the
officials on that question. In response we are providing this formal advice letter with further
explanation of our prior conclusion.

     Please note that we are only providing assistance under the conflict of interest provisions of
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

     Must Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse recuse themselves from a decision to
ask the Planning Commission to expedite the proposed ordinance regarding the Hillside area of the
city?

CONCLUSION

     Directing the Planning Commission to prioritize the timing of the development of an
ordinance, without affecting the content of the proposed ordinance, would appear to be merely
procedural and would not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember or their
property.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-018a
Page No. 2

FACTS

Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse (the "councilmembers") are two members of
the five-member City Council of the City of Beverly Hills. Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember
Bosse both own homes located in the Hillside Area of the City, which is an area generally located
north of Sunset Boulevard. The homes are their primary residences.

The Hillside Area of the City is a single-family zoning designation that has unique
development standards. The development standards are the same for every home in the Hillside
Area. The Hillside Area makes up 12% of the residential properties in the City and 5% of the
dwelling units in the City (due to a substantial number of multi family dwelling units in the City).

The Existing Central Area Ordinance: The Beverly Hills Municipal Code currently
requires design review of certain single family homes that are proposed to be constructed or
remodeled in the Central Area of the City, which is the area of the City other than the Hillside Area
and Trousdale Estates ("Central Area").

Pursuant to the design review ordinance for the Central Area of the City, if a home complies
with a pure architectural style as outlined in the City's adopted "style guide," describing certain
styles of homes that are typical in Beverly Hills, the project is subject to a staff level (Track 1)
review that is limited to determining consistency with the style guide. Projects involving portions
of the house visible from the public right-of-way that do not comply with the style guide
must undergo design review, involving consideration of the project by the City's Design Review
Commission (also referred to as Track 2).

If the alterations to the house involve only portions of the house that cannot be seen from the
public right-of-way, the house does not require any design review. The goals of requiring design
review of such homes include minimizing the appearance of scale and mass, enhancing the garden
quality of the City, and balancing the development expectations of the owner with the reasonable
expectation of privacy by the neighbors.

The Hillside Ordinance (yet to be developed): In consideration of the Central Area
ordinance which will be the model for the Hillside ordinance, you speculate:

• That the Hillside Area design review regulations might have a negative impact on the
council members' property value only if a council member redeveloped their property in a
manner that would trigger the design review ordinance (and it is your understanding that
neither intends to do so).

• The Hillside ordinance might have a positive impact on property only if the ordinance was
being applied to the property of their neighbors.

• Adoption of a Hillside Area design review ordinance would not change the development
potential of the property, and to the extent that it affects the property's zoning, that zoning
decision would affect the entire Hillside Area.

File No. A-17-018a
Page No. 3

• Further, the effect of any change in the zoning would likely be limited to requiring an additional review process and permit approval from the City for certain single family residential projects, as in the case in the Central Area.

In a correspondence dated February 8, 2017, you provided the following additional facts:

• The proposed Hillside ordinance has not yet been drafted, therefore it is not yet known whether it will be the same as the Central Area ordinance. You presume it will be substantially similar.

• The purpose of design review would be to look at certain houses which may have an adverse impact on the surrounding community. It is not intended to change the zoning restrictions.

• You do not believe the ordinance will lead to changing the development limits in the Hillside area. You stated that the purpose of design review would be to look at certain houses which have an adverse impact on the surrounding community. It is not intended to change the zoning restrictions.

ANALYSIS

In the Weiner Advice Letter, No. I-17-018, we informally advised that the Councilmembers' decision to direct the Planning Commission to prioritize the timing of the development of the Hillside ordinance, without discussing the content of the proposed ordinance, would be merely procedural and would not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember.

As noted in that prior letter, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers.

Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are set forth in Section 87103. The interest pertinent to the officials in question is set forth in Section 87103(b): "An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more." (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

In order for an interest in real property to be disqualifying, the specific decision in question must have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the property. A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect

File No. A-17-018a
Page No. 4

of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest
is material whenever the governmental decision:

"(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general (except as
provided below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed
boundaries of the plan;

"(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning
decision applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or
de-annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other
local government subdivision, or other boundaries, other than elective district
boundaries as determined by the California Citizen's Redistricting Commission or
any other agency where the governmental decision is to determine boundaries for
elective purposes;

"(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that
apply to the parcel;

"(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel;

"(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the
parcel or any variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on,
that real property. For purposes of this paragraph, any financial effect resulting
from a governmental decision regarding permits or licenses issued to the official's
business entity when operating on the official's real property shall be conclusively
analyzed under Regulation 18702.1, rather than this paragraph, without any
separate consideration for any material financial effects on the official's real
property as a result of the decision;

"(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an
interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from
improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly
situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise
receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision."

In all other cases, the financial effect is foreseeable if the financial effect can be recognized
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

The limited "procedural" decision you describe, to expedite the completion of the proposed
ordinance (without affecting the content of the ordinance itself) would appear to only have
hypothetical or theoretical affects on the official's real property. These "procedural" decisions,
while not explicitly exempt from the definition of "making, participating in making, or using his or
her position to influence" a governmental decision would ultimately have no financial effect on the
officials.

File No. A-17-018a
Page No. 5

We have addressed procedural decisions in the past, for example:

• "[M]aking a purely procedural decision, such as scheduling a court date, is not making or
participating in making a governmental decision. Advice, recommendations or
communications to fellow members of the city council made with the intent to influence the
outcome of a vote on a matter concerning litigation against the City or its public officials
would comprise participating in making, or influencing, a governmental decision."
(Battersby Advice Letter, No. I-02-141, emphasis added.)

• "We have .... advised that a purely procedural decision, such as stipulating to a date for a
hearing, will not have a foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest."
(Aladjem Advice Letter, No. A-99-111.)

On the other hand, as noted in Battersby, in cases where a procedural decision will itself
determine or affect the determination of a decision in which the official has a conflict of interest,
the official would have a conflict of interest in the procedural decision. For example:

• "[W]hile the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not apply to prevent an official from
participating in a decision that would not foreseeably have a material financial effect on any
of the official's economic interests, it would seem that decision to hold a special meeting
will have a material financial effect on your property because your property is directly
involved in the High Speed Rail decision and the special meeting would be for the purpose
of considering a legal challenge to the EIR supporting the High Speed Rail Project." (Cohen
Advice Letter, No. A-08-134.)

However, merely directing the Planning Commission to prioritize the task of writing the
ordinance (without affecting the content of the proposed ordinance) would appear to be merely
procedural and would not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

sections.question:
Must Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse recuse themselves from a decision to 
ask the Planning Commission to expedite the proposed ordinance regarding the Hillside area of the 
city?

Directing the Planning Commission to prioritize the timing of the development of an 
ordinance, without affecting the content of the proposed ordinance, would appear to be merely 
procedural and would not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember or their 
property.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse recuse themselves from a decision to 
ask the Planning Commission to expedite the proposed ordinance regarding the Hillside area of the 
city?

CONCLUSION: Directing the Planning Commission to prioritize the timing of the development of an 
ordinance, without affecting the content of the proposed ordinance, would appear to be merely 
procedural and would not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember or their 
property.
